ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and tangible new perspective by reducing slot-filling relation extraction to reading-comprehension via natural-language questions, enabling zero-shot extraction of relations specified only at test time. It also proposes a non-trivial technical extension (modeling answerability in a BiDAF-style reader) and a practical data-construction technique (schema querification + distant supervision) to cheaply generate large training sets. These contributions are clearly differentiated from prior work (per the summary) and would inform experts, though they combine existing components rather than introducing wholly unprecedented algorithms, so the novelty is substantial but not revolutionary. | B(4): The paper advances a notable new perspective by reframing slot-filling relation extraction as reading-comprehension (associating relation slots with natural-language questions) and demonstrates zero-shot extraction for relations specified only at test time. It also introduces a non-trivial technical extension (augmenting BiDAF-style models to model answerability) and a practical data-collection method (schema querification + distant supervision yielding 30M examples for $1,250). These elements collectively differentiate it from prior work and would inform experts, though the core components (QA models, distant supervision) leverage established techniques rather than being wholly unprecedented.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and tangible new perspective by reducing slot-filling relation extraction to reading-comprehension via natural-language questions, enabling zero-shot extraction of relations specified only at test time. It also proposes a non-trivial technical extension (modeling answerability in a BiDAF-style reader) and a practical data-construction technique (schema querification + distant supervision) to cheaply generate large training sets. These contributions are clearly differentiated from prior work (per the summary) and would inform experts, though they combine existing components rather than introducing wholly unprecedented algorithms, so the novelty is substantial but not revolutionary.

It also introduces a non-trivial technical extension (augmenting BiDAF-style models to model answerability) and a practical data-collection method (schema querification + distant supervision yielding 30M examples for $1,250). These elements collectively differentiate it from prior work and would inform experts, though the core components (QA models, distant supervision) leverage established techniques rather than being wholly unprecedented.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of scalable and flexible relation extraction by reducing slot-filling to reading-comprehension questions and demonstrating zero-shot extraction of relations specified only at test time. Its combination of schema querification with cheap distant supervision (30M examples for $1,250) and an extended BiDAF-style model that models answerability can change how practitioners formulate and train relation extractors. While pure zero-shot performance is lower and some errors stem from annotation/span issues, the approach opens significant new directions for QA-based IE and is likely to be widely adopted and built upon across NLP subareas. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem by reframing relation extraction as reading comprehension and demonstrating practical zero-shot extraction, which could substantially change how researchers approach slot-filling (mapping relations to natural-language questions and extending BiDAF for answerability). The ability to cheaply create very large training sets (30M examples for $1,250) and to generalize to new questions for known relations suggests strong practical impact and likelihood of follow-up work. Limitations—lower accuracy in the pure zero-shot setting and noise in distant supervision—temper its transformative potential but do not preclude substantial influence across relation extraction and QA-based information extraction research.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of scalable and flexible relation extraction by reducing slot-filling to reading-comprehension questions and demonstrating zero-shot extraction of relations specified only at test time. Its combination of schema querification with cheap distant supervision (30M examples for $1,250) and an extended BiDAF-style model that models answerability can change how practitioners formulate and train relation extractors. While pure zero-shot performance is lower and some errors stem from annotation/span issues, the approach opens significant new directions for QA-based IE and is likely to be widely adopted and built upon across NLP subareas.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important problem by reframing relation extraction as reading comprehension and demonstrating practical zero-shot extraction, which could substantially change how researchers approach slot-filling (mapping relations to natural-language questions and extending BiDAF for answerability). The ability to cheaply create very large training sets (30M examples for $1,250) and to generalize to new questions for known relations suggests strong practical impact and likelihood of follow-up work. Limitations—lower accuracy in the pure zero-shot setting and noise in distant supervision—temper its transformative potential but do not preclude substantial influence across relation extraction and QA-based information extraction research.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The summary indicates no baselines were mentioned for empirical comparison despite using standard datasets (WikiReading/Wikidata) and common metrics (Precision/Recall/F1), so key comparative evaluations are missing. While the paper thoroughly covers related work and builds on reading-comprehension models like BiDAF, it does not report head-to-head results against appropriate recent baselines or prior relation-extraction methods. This makes the experimental comparisons inadequate for judging relative performance. | B(1): The summary states that no baselines were mentioned, so the paper provides no empirical comparisons to prior methods despite using standard datasets (WikiReading/Wikidata) and common metrics (P/R/F1). This means fairness of comparisons cannot be assessed and the paper does not demonstrate whether prior methods perform comparably or better. Although the related-work discussion is described as thorough, the lack of quantified baseline comparisons makes the empirical evaluation non-meaningful relative to prior work.

reviewer_a.justification: The summary indicates no baselines were mentioned for empirical comparison despite using standard datasets (WikiReading/Wikidata) and common metrics (Precision/Recall/F1), so key comparative evaluations are missing. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's central claims are generally supported by empirical evidence: mapping relations to natural-language questions and extending a BiDAF-style reader to model answerability are validated by experiments (including reported strong generalization to new questions for known relations), and the large-scale training set claim is substantiated by producing 30M examples for $1,250. However, the summary indicates no mentioned baseline comparisons or statistical testing, and nontrivial annotation/span-selection errors are reported, which weakens confidence in some experimental conclusions. These are minor methodological concerns that do not overturn the main results, hence a score of 4. | B(3): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and backed by experiments (extension of BiDAF to model answerability, large-scale dataset creation, and reported zero-shot results), and there are no mathematical derivations to check. However, the experimental methodology appears incomplete: no baselines are mentioned and statistical testing/controls are not reported, weakening evidence for comparative effectiveness. Some conclusions (especially about zero-shot generalization) are acknowledged as limited in the paper and supported only at lower accuracy, so overall the work is promising but has methodological gaps that prevent full technical validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's central claims are generally supported by empirical evidence: mapping relations to natural-language questions and extending a BiDAF-style reader to model answerability are validated by experiments (including reported strong generalization to new questions for known relations), and the large-scale training set claim is substantiated by producing 30M examples for $1,250. However, the summary indicates no mentioned baseline comparisons or statistical testing, and nontrivial annotation/span-selection errors are reported, which weakens confidence in some experimental conclusions. These are minor methodological concerns that do not overturn the main results, hence a score of 4.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and backed by experiments (extension of BiDAF to model answerability, large-scale dataset creation, and reported zero-shot results), and there are no mathematical derivations to check. However, the experimental methodology appears incomplete: no baselines are mentioned and statistical testing/controls are not reported, weakening evidence for comparative effectiveness. Some conclusions (especially about zero-shot generalization) are acknowledged as limited in the paper and supported only at lower accuracy, so overall the work is promising but has methodological gaps that prevent full technical validation.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (relation extraction) and proposes a novel, well-positioned formulation (reducing slot filling to reading comprehension) that will interest a top-tier conference audience. It uses standard datasets (WikiReading/Wikidata), appropriate metrics (Precision/Recall/F1), and realistic experimental claims (zero-shot evaluation, large distant-supervised training set, hyperparameters reported), with thorough related work and clear limitations. One minor concern from the summary is that no baselines were mentioned, which could affect experimental evaluation strength but does not undermine topical fit. | B(5): Yes. The paper addresses core NLP/ML topics (relation extraction, reading comprehension, and zero-shot learning) and targets the right audience for a top-tier venue. Its formulation—reducing slot-filling to QA and extending BiDAF-style models to model answerability—is appropriate and aligns with current neural methods, and the large-scale training via schema querification plus distant supervision (30M examples) is a relevant engineering contribution. Evaluation uses standard datasets (WikiReading/Wikidata) and metrics (Precision/Recall/F1) and the related-work coverage and stated limitations indicate proper positioning within the community.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (relation extraction) and proposes a novel, well-positioned formulation (reducing slot filling to reading comprehension) that will interest a top-tier conference audience. It uses standard datasets (WikiReading/Wikidata), appropriate metrics (Precision/Recall/F1), and realistic experimental claims (zero-shot evaluation, large distant-supervised training set, hyperparameters reported), with thorough related work and clear limitations. One minor concern from the summary is that no baselines were mentioned, which could affect experimental evaluation strength but does not undermine topical fit.

Evaluation uses standard datasets (WikiReading/Wikidata) and metrics (Precision/Recall/F1) and the related-work coverage and stated limitations indicate proper positioning within the community.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear logical flow from the problem framing (reducing slot-filling to reading comprehension) through the method (extended BiDAF modeling answerability) to experiments (large-scale distant-supervision data, zero-shot evaluation) and conclusions. The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is reported as good; reporting of hyperparameters and thorough related-work coverage further support clarity. However, the provided summary does not mention figures/tables or explicit notation details, so there may be minor presentation gaps in illustrative material and formal definitions. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized and presents its main contributions early (reducing relation extraction to reading-comprehension questions and extending BiDAF for answerability), and the methodology and datasets are described clearly in the summary. Notation/use of experimental details seems consistent (hyperparameters reported) and related work coverage is thorough; overall writing quality is noted as good. One minor uncertainty is that the summary does not indicate whether figures, tables, and examples are particularly effective, so there is a small open question about presentation polish.

The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is reported as good; reporting of hyperparameters and thorough related-work coverage further support clarity. Notation/use of experimental details seems consistent (hyperparameters reported) and related work coverage is thorough; overall writing quality is noted as good. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides substantial empirical evidence for its claims: it trains an extended BiDAF-style reader on a large (30M-example) dataset built via schema querification + distant supervision and reports precision/recall/F1 with zero-shot experiments demonstrating generalization. The authors include error analysis (e.g., 36% annotation errors in a 50-example sample, 42% span-selection errors), report hyperparameters, and cover related work thoroughly. However, the summary notes no baselines were mentioned and ablation/sensitivity studies are not reported, and code availability is not stated, which leaves some reproducibility and comparative-evaluation gaps—thus a solid but not exhaustive evaluation. | B(3): The paper provides substantial experimental evidence for its main claims (large-scale training data, extended BiDAF modeling, and zero-shot results), and discusses limitations and error analysis (annotation errors, span-selection issues, and lower accuracy for unseen relations). However, no baselines are mentioned in the summary and ablation/sensitivity analyses are not reported, leaving gaps in assessing relative and component-wise contributions. Reproducibility is partially supported (dataset construction method and hyperparameters reported), but code is not mentioned, which may hinder exact replication.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides substantial empirical evidence for its claims: it trains an extended BiDAF-style reader on a large (30M-example) dataset built via schema querification + distant supervision and reports precision/recall/F1 with zero-shot experiments demonstrating generalization. The authors include error analysis (e.g., 36% annotation errors in a 50-example sample, 42% span-selection errors), report hyperparameters, and cover related work thoroughly. However, the summary notes no baselines were mentioned and ablation/sensitivity studies are not reported, and code availability is not stated, which leaves some reproducibility and comparative-evaluation gaps—thus a solid but not exhaustive evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides substantial experimental evidence for its main claims (large-scale training data, extended BiDAF modeling, and zero-shot results), and discusses limitations and error analysis (annotation errors, span-selection issues, and lower accuracy for unseen relations). However, no baselines are mentioned in the summary and ablation/sensitivity analyses are not reported, leaving gaps in assessing relative and component-wise contributions. Reproducibility is partially supported (dataset construction method and hyperparameters reported), but code is not mentioned, which may hinder exact replication.

main_claims: Relation extraction can be reduced to answering simple reading-comprehension questions by associating one or more natural-language questions with each relation slot.
By extending recent neural reading-comprehension techniques (e.g., BiDAF) to model answerability, one can learn high-quality relation-extraction models.

Large training sets can be built cheaply by combining relation-level crowd-sourced questions (schema querification) with distant supervision (they generated 30,000,000 examples with a budget of $1,250).

Zero-shot learning is possible: the approach can extract new relations specified only at test time (no labeled training examples), and experiments show generalization to new questions for known relations with high accuracy and to unseen relations at lower accuracy.
_summary.methodology: Reduce slot-filling relation extraction to reading comprehension by mapping relation slots to natural-language questions and training an extended BiDAF-style reading-comprehension model that models answerability; training data are created via schema querification and distant supervision using Wikidata/WikiReading.
limitations: Zero-shot generalization to unseen relations achieves lower accuracy than for known relations.
A notable fraction of errors are due to annotation errors in Wikidata (36% in a 50-example sample).
Many errors stem from inaccurate span selection (42% in a 50-example sample), which penalizes the model even when it finds the correct information.
The “pure” zero-shot scenario (no manifestation of the test relation observed at train time) is substantially more challenging.
